Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP)

Interactive Hematology Reference — PGY-1 Level

Peripheral smear: schistocytes (arrows) — hallmark of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (Wikimedia Commons)
High-magnification TTP blood smear with schistocytes (Wikimedia Commons)
Pathophysiology Clinical Labs PLASMIC Score Differential Management Monitoring Special Populations Pearls
1

Pathophysiology

ADAMTS13 deficiency leads to ultra-large vWF multimer accumulation, spontaneous platelet adhesion, microthrombi, and mechanical RBC shearing.
ADAMTS13 <10% UL-vWF Multimers Platelet-Rich Microthrombi Untreated: >90% mortality

Detailed Molecular Mechanism

Watch TTP Pathophysiology on Osmosis
Recommended: Osmosis video with detailed animated pathophysiology illustrations
ADAMTS13/VWF Cascade: Normal cleavage (green) vs TTP pathway (red) — antibody-mediated ADAMTS13 inhibition leads to platelet microthrombi and RBC shearing

ADAMTS13: The Central Enzyme

ADAMTS13 (A Disintegrin And Metalloprotease with ThromboSpondin type 1 motifs, member 13) is a zinc-containing metalloprotease synthesized primarily by hepatic stellate cells, with minor contributions from endothelial cells, podocytes, and megakaryocytes. It is the sole enzyme responsible for cleaving ultra-large von Willebrand Factor (UL-vWF) multimers at the Tyr1605–Met1606 bond within the A2 domain.

ADAMTS13/VWF mechanism: VWF conformation, unraveling, domain structure, and cleavage activation. Source: J Clin Med 2021;10(3):536, CC BY 4.0

Domain Structure

ADAMTS13 is a large, multi-domain protein (~190 kDa) with a modular architecture critical to its function. From N-terminus to C-terminus, the domains are:

  • Metalloprotease domain: Contains the catalytic zinc-binding site (HEXXHXXGXXHD motif). This is the active site that cleaves vWF. Requires a calcium ion for structural stability.
  • Disintegrin-like domain: Immediately C-terminal to the metalloprotease domain. Together they form the catalytic unit. Critical for substrate recognition and positioning.
  • Thrombospondin type 1 (TSP1) repeat: The first TSP1 repeat follows the cysteine-rich and spacer domains. A total of 8 TSP1 repeats are present, contributing to vWF binding, protein folding, and secretion.
  • Cysteine-rich domain: Participates in an exosite that recognizes the D4-CK domains of vWF, enabling the enzyme to "walk" along the vWF multimer.
  • Spacer domain: A major antigenic target — most autoantibodies in acquired TTP target the spacer domain (residues Arg568, Phe592, Arg660, Tyr661, Tyr665). This domain also makes critical contacts with the vWF A2 domain.
  • CUB domains (CUB1 and CUB2): Two C-terminal CUB domains regulate enzyme conformation. In circulation, ADAMTS13 exists in a "closed" conformation where the CUB domains fold back onto the spacer domain, autoinhibiting the enzyme. Binding to vWF or shear stress opens this conformation. Non-neutralizing autoantibodies also target CUB domains, accelerating clearance.

Mechanism of Action: Shear-Dependent Cleavage

Under normal conditions, the A2 domain of vWF is buried within the globular structure of the protein and inaccessible to ADAMTS13. Cleavage requires a conformational change:

ADAMTS13 domain structure. Source: IJMS 2024;25(15):8137, CC BY 4.0
  • Shear stress in arterioles (typically >5,000/s) physically unfurls the vWF multimer, exposing the A2 domain cleavage site.
  • ADAMTS13 binds vWF through multiple exosites: the spacer domain contacts the vWF A2 domain, while distal TSP1 repeats and CUB domains engage the D4-CK region.
  • The metalloprotease domain then cleaves the Tyr1605–Met1606 bond, converting UL-vWF into smaller, hemostatic multimers.
  • This process occurs both on the endothelial surface (where newly secreted UL-vWF is anchored) and in flowing blood under shear.

Key Parameters

  • Normal activity: 50–150% (0.5–1.5 IU/mL)
  • Half-life: ~2–3 days in plasma
  • Diagnostic cutoff: <10% activity is the established threshold for severe deficiency. Below this level, UL-vWF multimers accumulate to a concentration sufficient to cause spontaneous platelet adhesion even without vascular injury. Activity 10–20% represents a gray zone; patients may present during physiologic stress (surgery, infection, pregnancy) when vWF levels rise.
  • Why <10%? Kinetic studies show that at 10% residual activity, the rate of UL-vWF cleavage falls below the rate of endothelial secretion, allowing net multimer accumulation. This threshold was validated in large cohort studies (Oklahoma TTP Registry, French Reference Center) showing >95% PPV for TTP diagnosis.
ADAMTS13 mechanism of action — cleavage of UL-vWF (Kavanagh et al., J Clin Med, CC BY 4.0)
ADAMTS13 investigation flowchart (J Clin Med, CC BY 4.0)
Without ADAMTS13 cleavage, UL-vWF multimers remain anchored to endothelium and unfurl into flowing blood, creating massive strings that spontaneously capture platelets even without vascular injury. A single UL-vWF string can capture >100 platelets within seconds under flow conditions.

Congenital TTP (Upshaw-Schulman Syndrome)

Congenital TTP, also known as Upshaw-Schulman syndrome (USS), is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by biallelic loss-of-function mutations in the ADAMTS13 gene located on chromosome 9q34. It accounts for approximately 5% of all TTP cases but is likely underdiagnosed, particularly in adults with mild phenotypes.

Genetics

Over 200 distinct mutations have been identified across the ADAMTS13 gene, including missense, nonsense, splice-site, and frameshift mutations. Mutations are distributed throughout the gene without a single hotspot, though certain mutations are more prevalent in specific populations:

  • c.4143dupA (p.Glu1382Argfs*6): Most common in European populations
  • R1060W: Relatively common missense mutation affecting the TSP1 repeats
  • Compound heterozygosity is more common than homozygosity in most patients
  • Heterozygous carriers: ADAMTS13 activity 40–70%, clinically asymptomatic but important for genetic counseling

Clinical Presentation

The phenotype is remarkably variable, even within families sharing the same mutations, reflecting the influence of environmental triggers on disease expression:

  • Neonatal presentation (~25%): Severe neonatal jaundice, thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anemia requiring exchange transfusion. Often misdiagnosed as neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia or sepsis.
  • Childhood presentation (~50%): Recurrent episodes triggered by infections, often diagnosed after multiple TMA episodes. Chronic mild hemolysis between episodes.
  • Adult-onset (~25%): First episode during pregnancy (especially first pregnancy) or other physiologic stress. May be misdiagnosed as HELLP, preeclampsia, or acquired TTP.

Diagnosis

  • ADAMTS13 activity: Constitutively <5–10%
  • Key distinguishing feature: NO inhibitory antibodies (anti-ADAMTS13 IgG negative, Bethesda inhibitor absent)
  • Genetic testing: Confirms diagnosis; identifies specific mutations for family screening
  • Family history: Often negative (autosomal recessive) — absence of family history does NOT exclude USS

Treatment: Regular FFP Infusions

The treatment of congenital TTP is fundamentally different from acquired TTP because there are no autoantibodies to remove:

  • Acute episodes: FFP infusion 10–15 mL/kg (provides exogenous ADAMTS13)
  • Prophylaxis: Regular FFP infusions every 2–4 weeks, titrated to prevent episodes. Given the ~2–3 day half-life of ADAMTS13, trough levels may be very low, but clinical protection persists longer than expected (possibly due to upfront cleavage of accumulated UL-vWF).
  • TPE is NOT indicated — there are no antibodies to remove. Plasma exchange wastes the ADAMTS13 just infused. This is a board-relevant distinction.
  • Recombinant ADAMTS13 (TAK-755/BAX930): In clinical trials (phase 3). Would eliminate infection risk from plasma products and allow precise dosing.

Pregnancy Management in Congenital TTP

Pregnancy is particularly dangerous because vWF levels physiologically rise 2–3x during gestation, overwhelming the already-deficient ADAMTS13:

  • Preconception planning: Confirm diagnosis, establish baseline ADAMTS13 activity
  • Prophylactic FFP: Increase frequency to every 1–2 weeks starting in the 2nd trimester
  • Monitor closely: Platelet count, LDH, haptoglobin at each visit
  • Delivery: Often induced at 36–38 weeks to minimize maternal risk
  • Postpartum: Continue FFP prophylaxis for 4–6 weeks (vWF levels remain elevated)
Pregnancy is a common trigger for congenital TTP because vWF levels physiologically rise 2–3x during gestation, overwhelming the already-deficient ADAMTS13. Any woman with recurrent pregnancy-associated TMA and negative ADAMTS13 inhibitor should be evaluated for USS.

Acquired TTP: Autoimmune Destruction (>95% of cases)

Acquired TTP is an autoimmune disease caused by IgG autoantibodies (predominantly IgG4 and IgG1 subclasses) directed against ADAMTS13. These antibodies cause severe ADAMTS13 deficiency (<10%) through two mechanisms: direct neutralization of catalytic activity and accelerated clearance from plasma.

Autoantibody Characteristics

  • Neutralizing (inhibitory) antibodies: Directly block catalytic activity by binding the spacer domain (most commonly targeting residues Arg568, Phe592, Arg660, Tyr661, Tyr665) and, to a lesser extent, the metalloprotease and cysteine-rich domains. Measured in Bethesda units (BU).
  • Non-neutralizing antibodies: Bind the CUB domains and TSP1 repeats. Do not directly block enzymatic activity but accelerate plasma clearance of ADAMTS13, effectively reducing its half-life from ~2–3 days to hours. Measured by anti-ADAMTS13 IgG ELISA.
  • Inhibitor >2 BU: High burden → associated with higher relapse risk, potential refractory disease, and need for early rituximab
  • Anti-ADAMTS13 IgG ELISA is more sensitive than functional inhibitor assay (~90% vs ~80% sensitivity) for detecting autoantibodies

Triggers & Associations

In approximately 50% of cases, no trigger is identified (idiopathic). Identified triggers and associations include:

CategoryExamplesNotes
InfectionsHIV, hepatitis C, H. pylori, CMVHIV is the most well-documented; TTP may be the presenting manifestation. H. pylori molecular mimicry may trigger anti-ADAMTS13 antibodies.
Pregnancy2nd/3rd trimester, postpartumAccounts for 10–25% of TTP episodes in women. Must distinguish from HELLP/preeclampsia (ADAMTS13 <10% = TTP). Postpartum onset may occur up to 8 weeks after delivery.
AutoimmuneSLE (10–15%), RA, scleroderma, antiphospholipid syndromeSLE is the strongest association. TTP may precede SLE diagnosis by years. Shared autoimmune diathesis.
DrugsTiclopidine, clopidogrel, quinineTiclopidine: true autoimmune TTP (ADAMTS13 <10%) in ~1:1600 exposed. Clopidogrel: rare. Quinine: usually immune-mediated TMA with normal ADAMTS13 (different mechanism).
MalignancyVarious (rare)May unmask or trigger TTP
TransplantBMT, solid organCalcineurin inhibitor-associated TMA often has normal ADAMTS13 (different mechanism)

Racial and Genetic Susceptibility

African Americans are at ~9-fold increased risk of acquired TTP compared to Caucasians. This disparity is mediated by HLA associations:

  • HLA-DRB1*11 (especially HLA-DRB1*11:04): Present in ~70% of Black patients with acquired TTP vs ~20% of controls. This HLA allele presents ADAMTS13-derived peptides (particularly from the CUB2 domain) to CD4+ T cells, facilitating autoantibody production.
  • Additional associations: HLA-DQB1*03 (in linkage disequilibrium with DRB1*11)
  • The HLA association explains not only the racial disparity but also the higher relapse rate observed in Black patients (~50% vs ~30% in Caucasians)
The combination of African American race + young/middle-aged woman + thrombocytopenia + MAHA should place TTP at the very top of the differential. The 9x racial disparity is HLA-driven (DRB1*11) and is frequently tested on boards.
Immune vs hereditary TTP mechanisms. Source: J Clin Med 2023;12(9):3111, CC BY 4.0

The Microvascular Thrombosis Cascade

When ADAMTS13 activity falls below 10%, the rate of UL-vWF cleavage drops below the rate of endothelial secretion, leading to net accumulation of UL-vWF multimers on the endothelial surface and in flowing blood. The following biophysical cascade then unfolds:

Step 1: UL-vWF Multimer Accumulation

UL-vWF multimers are massive proteins exceeding 20,000 kDa (compared to ~500–10,000 kDa for normal plasma vWF). They are secreted from Weibel-Palade bodies of endothelial cells and, in the absence of ADAMTS13, remain anchored to the cell surface via P-selectin. These strings can extend hundreds of micrometers, literally spanning the lumen of arterioles.

Step 2: Shear-Dependent Unfurling

Arterioles generate high shear stress (>5,000/s), which physically unfurls the globular UL-vWF multimers into elongated strings. This conformational change is critical because it:

  • Exposes the A1 domain of vWF, the platelet-binding domain
  • Would normally also expose the A2 domain for ADAMTS13 cleavage — but in TTP, no functional ADAMTS13 is present
  • The degree of unfurling is proportional to shear stress, explaining why arterioles (high shear) are preferentially affected over veins (low shear)

Step 3: Platelet Capture and Activation

The exposed A1 domain of unfurled vWF binds the GPIb-IX-V complex on platelets. This is the same receptor used in normal primary hemostasis, but in TTP it occurs pathologically and spontaneously without vascular injury:

  • Initial platelet binding via GPIb-IX-V is a catch bond — paradoxically strengthened by shear force
  • Bound platelets become activated, releasing their granule contents (ADP, thromboxane A2, more vWF from alpha granules)
  • This creates a positive feedback loop: activated platelets release more vWF, which unfurls and captures more platelets
  • Activated platelets also express GPIIb/IIIa, cross-linking with fibrinogen to form a platelet aggregate

Step 4: Microvascular Obstruction

The growing platelet-vWF aggregate creates a fibrin-poor, platelet-rich "white clot" that progressively narrows the arteriolar lumen. Key distinctions:

  • "White clots" (platelet-rich) are pathognomonic of TTP — distinct from "red clots" (fibrin/RBC-rich) of DIC
  • Coagulation cascade is NOT activated → PT, PTT, and fibrinogen all remain NORMAL (this distinguishes TTP from DIC)
  • D-dimer may be mildly elevated from secondary fibrinolysis but is not dramatically raised as in DIC
  • Platelet consumption is mechanical/thrombotic — NOT immune destruction (unlike ITP where anti-platelet antibodies cause splenic sequestration)

Step 5: Mechanical RBC Shearing

As arteriolar lumens narrow, RBCs are forced through the fibrin-platelet mesh at high velocity, causing mechanical fragmentation:

  • Schistocytes are the hallmark — fragmented RBCs taking characteristic shapes: helmet cells, triangle cells, microspherocytes, and crescent forms
  • Schistocyte percentage >1% on peripheral smear is significant; TTP typically shows 2–5%+
  • RBC destruction releases LDH (markedly elevated, often >1000 U/L) and free hemoglobin
  • Haptoglobin is consumed by binding free hemoglobin → undetectable
  • This process is Coombs-negative (no immune component) — the hemolysis is purely mechanical
Platelet transfusion = "fuel to the fire." Adding platelets provides more substrate for microthrombi, worsening organ damage and potentially causing death. Platelet transfusion is contraindicated unless there is life-threatening hemorrhage or urgent surgery.
TTP pathologic overview: triggers → ADAMTS13 dysfunction → microthrombi → organ damage. Source: Biomedicines 2024;12(3):621, CC BY 4.0

End-Organ Damage & Hemolysis

Microthrombi act as a mechanical sieve. RBCs forced through narrowed microvasculature are sheared into schistocytes. This is MAHA: mechanical, non-immune (Coombs-negative). The pattern of organ injury in TTP reflects the distribution and shear characteristics of the microvasculature.

End-Organ Microthrombi: Central arteriolar occlusion radiating to organ-specific damage patterns with prevalence

Brain (Most Common, 60–70%)

The brain is preferentially affected because cerebral arterioles have among the highest shear stress in the body. Microthrombi form in cortical and subcortical arterioles, causing:

  • Fluctuating neurologic symptoms — the hallmark of TTP. Symptoms wax and wane because microthrombi dynamically form and lyse. A patient may have confusion one hour and be lucid the next. This pattern is virtually pathognomonic.
  • Headache (~50%): Often severe, may mimic migraine or subarachnoid hemorrhage
  • Confusion/altered mental status (~40%): Ranges from subtle cognitive changes to delirium
  • Focal deficits (~20%): Transient hemiparesis, aphasia, visual changes — may be misdiagnosed as TIA/stroke
  • Seizures (~10–15%): Focal or generalized; associated with worse prognosis
  • Coma (<10%): Late finding; associated with high mortality if untreated
  • MRI findings: Often normal early; may show scattered cortical/subcortical microinfarcts, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) pattern, or white matter changes

Heart (25–50%)

Cardiac involvement is increasingly recognized as a major cause of mortality in TTP and is often subclinical:

  • Troponin elevation: Present in 25–50% of acute TTP episodes. Results from myocardial microthrombi causing patchy subendocardial ischemia.
  • ECG changes: ST-segment changes, T-wave inversions, arrhythmias (especially sinus tachycardia, atrial fibrillation)
  • Sudden cardiac death: Accounts for up to 25% of TTP mortality. Often due to ventricular arrhythmias from myocardial microinfarction or acute heart failure.
  • Cardiomyopathy: Takotsubo-like pattern or diffuse hypokinesis from microvascular injury
  • Clinical pearl: Obtain troponin and ECG on ALL TTP patients. Troponin >0.25 ng/mL is associated with higher mortality and may warrant ICU monitoring.

Kidney (50%)

Renal involvement in TTP is usually mild compared to HUS, and this distinction is diagnostically important:

  • Creatinine: Typically 1.5–2.5 mg/dL. Severe AKI (Cr >3.0) suggests HUS or aHUS rather than TTP.
  • Proteinuria and hematuria: Common but mild
  • Why mild? Renal arterioles have lower shear stress than cerebral arterioles (afferent arterioles dilate to regulate GFR), so less UL-vWF unfurling and platelet adhesion occurs. In contrast, HUS involves direct endothelial toxicity (Shiga toxin) which preferentially damages glomerular endothelium.
  • Dialysis: Rarely needed (<10% of TTP; >50% of typical HUS)

Gastrointestinal

  • Abdominal pain (~30%): From mesenteric and pancreatic microthrombi
  • Pancreatitis: Lipase elevation is common; clinical pancreatitis occurs in ~10%. Pancreatic microthrombi cause ischemic pancreatitis.
  • Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea: Non-specific but frequent

Adrenal (Rare)

  • Adrenal hemorrhage/insufficiency: Rare but reported. The adrenal glands have a rich arteriolar blood supply vulnerable to microthrombi.
  • Should be considered if refractory hypotension persists despite adequate TPE and volume resuscitation
  • Check random cortisol; treat empirically with stress-dose hydrocortisone if suspected
OrganFrequencyKey ManifestationPrognostic Note
Brain60–70%Fluctuating neuro (wax/wane)Seizures/coma = poor prognosis
Heart25–50%Troponin elevation, arrhythmiasLeading cause of acute mortality
Kidney~50%Mild AKI (Cr 1.5–2.5)Severe AKI suggests HUS, not TTP
GI~30%Abdominal pain, pancreatitisLipase elevation common
AdrenalRareHemorrhage/insufficiencyConsider if refractory hypotension
2

Clinical Presentation

Classic pentad present in <5% at diagnosis. Modern dx: thrombocytopenia + MAHA + no alternative explanation.
Pentad <5% Fluctuating Neuro F:M 3:1
Ecchymosis from low platelets in TTP (Wikimedia Commons)

Bruising and Petechiae

Severe thrombocytopenia (often <30,000/μL) in TTP causes mucocutaneous bleeding including petechiae, purpura, and ecchymoses. While not specific to TTP, the combination of bruising with MAHA and neurologic symptoms is highly suggestive. Petechiae are most prominent on dependent areas and oral mucosa.

Schistocytes (marked) in TTP (Wikimedia Commons)
Additional schistocyte examples (Wikimedia Commons)
RBC morphology reference (Wikimedia Commons)

The Classic Pentad (Historical — Present in <5%)

ComponentFrequencyKey Detail
1. Thrombocytopenia>95%Median ~10,000–20,000/mcL, almost always <30,000
2. MAHA>95%Schistocytes >1%, LDH often >1,000, haptoglobin undetectable
3. Neurological60–70%Headache, confusion, focal deficits, seizures (10–20%), fluctuating
4. Renal~50%Mild: Cr 1.5–2.5. If Cr >3–4 or dialysis → think HUS
5. Fever~25%Low-grade (37.8–38.5°C), non-specific
Do NOT wait for all five features. Thrombocytopenia + MAHA without alternative explanation = start TPE immediately.

Detailed Symptom Breakdown

FindingFrequencyDetails
Cardiac25–50%Troponin elevation, chest pain, arrhythmias, HF, sudden cardiac death. ECG: ST changes, QTc prolongation.
Abdominal30–40%N/V, abdominal pain, diarrhea. Pancreatitis (lipase elevation) in 15–20%.
Skin40–50%Petechiae, purpura, ecchymoses (thrombocytopenia). Jaundice (hemolysis).
ConstitutionalCommonFatigue, malaise, weakness — often initial symptoms.
Fluctuating neurological symptoms are nearly pathognomonic. Confused one hour, lucid the next, then seizes = TTP until proven otherwise. Reflects dynamic microthrombi formation and resolution.

ParameterValue
Incidence~3–6 per million/year

Peak age30–50 years (acquired); neonatal/childhood (congenital)

Untreated mortality>90%

Lab Findings

Normal coags (PT/PTT/fibrinogen) with thrombocytopenia + MAHA = TTP, not DIC. Draw ADAMTS13 before plasma exchange.
Schistocytes LDH >1000 Coombs Negative Normal Coags
NORMAL PT / PTT / FIBRINOGEN = NOT DIC — The single most important lab distinction and the most tested point.
ADAMTS13 investigation algorithm for TMA. Source: J Clin Med 2021;10(3):536, CC BY 4.0

Expected Lab Values in TTP

TestExpectedSignificance
Platelet count<30,000/mcL (median ~15,000)Consumption into microthrombi
Hemoglobin6–9 g/dLMAHA
WBCNormal or mildly elevatedHelps rule out leukemia/aplastic anemia
Peripheral smearSchistocytes (>1%, often 2–5%+), polychromasia, nRBCsMANDATORY — do not diagnose without it
LDHOften >1,000 U/L (sometimes >2,000)Hemolysis + tissue ischemia. Best daily marker.
HaptoglobinUsually undetectable (<10 mg/dL)Consumed binding free Hgb
Indirect bilirubin2–5 mg/dLHemolysis byproduct
Reticulocyte count>4–6% (absolute >120K/mcL)Appropriate marrow response
CreatinineMild: 1.5–2.5 mg/dLIf >3–4 → think HUS
PT / PTTNORMALKEY: distinguishes from DIC
FibrinogenNORMAL (often elevated as APR)KEY: low in DIC, normal in TTP
D-dimerNormal or mildly elevatedNot as dramatic as DIC
Direct CoombsNegativeRules out AIHA — mechanical hemolysis
ADAMTS13 activity<10% = diagnosticSEND BEFORE PLASMA EXCHANGE
ADAMTS13 inhibitorPositive in acquired TTP (Bethesda units)>2 BU = high inhibitor burden
Anti-ADAMTS13 IgGPositive in acquired TTPMore sensitive than functional inhibitor
TroponinElevated in 25–50%Check on ALL TTP patients
LipaseMay be elevatedPancreatic microthrombi

Critical Lab Interpretation Rules

1Normal PT/PTT/fibrinogen = NOT DIC. Coagulation cascade is not activated in TTP — only the platelet plug pathway is dysfunctional.
2Negative Coombs = NOT AIHA. TTP is mechanical hemolysis (shearing through platelet/vWF mesh), not antibody-mediated.
3Draw ADAMTS13 BEFORE plasma exchange. TPE normalizes ADAMTS13 levels, making the diagnostic test uninterpretable. Common exam pitfall.
4LDH is the best daily monitoring marker — reflects both hemolysis and tissue ischemia, responds faster than platelet count.
5If Cr >3–4 mg/dL or dialysis required → strongly reconsider TTP. Think HUS or aHUS instead.

PLASMIC Score Calculator

Predicts likelihood of severe ADAMTS13 deficiency (<10%). Guides empiric TPE initiation while awaiting ADAMTS13 results (2–5 day turnaround).
Score 6–7 = Start TPE PPV ~70%

Score Interpretation

ScoreRiskP(ADAMTS13 <10%)Action
0–4Low<5%TTP unlikely; investigate other TMA causes
5Intermediate~10–25%Clinical judgment; consider empiric TPE if high suspicion
6–7High~62–72%Start TPE empirically while awaiting ADAMTS13
MCV <90 excludes B12/folate deficiency mimics. INR <1.5 helps exclude DIC. Creatinine <2.0 helps exclude HUS.
5

Differential Diagnosis

TTP must be distinguished from other thrombotic microangiopathies and causes of thrombocytopenia with hemolysis.
TTP vs HUS vs DIC ADAMTS13 is Key

Comprehensive TMA Comparison

Feature TTP STEC-HUS aHUS DIC ITP HELLP Evans
MechanismADAMTS13 deficiencyShiga toxinComplement dysregSystemic coag activationAntiplatelet AbPregnancy endothelialAIHA + ITP

Pregnancy-Associated TMA Distinction

FeatureTTPHELLPPreeclampsiaaHUS
TimingAny trimester, postpartum>20 weeks, postpartum>20 weeksUsually postpartum
ADAMTS13<10%>10% (usually >30%)NormalNormal
Liver enzymesNormal/mildMarkedly elevatedMildNormal
HypertensionAbsent or mildPresentDefining featureMay be present
Response to deliveryNO improvementImproves 48–72hImproves 48–72hNO improvement
TreatmentTPEDeliveryDelivery + MgEculizumab
ADAMTS13 <10% = TTP, not HELLP. HELLP/preeclampsia improve after delivery; TTP does not. This is the key diagnostic and therapeutic distinction in pregnancy-associated TMA.
6

Management

Hematologic emergency requiring same-day TPE. Modern quad-therapy: TPE + steroids + caplacizumab + rituximab reduces mortality to <5%.
TPE = Cornerstone NO Platelet Transfusion Untreated: >90% mortality
Plasmapheresis setup for therapeutic plasma exchange (Wikimedia Commons)

Immediate Actions (Within Hours)

  1. DO NOT WAIT for ADAMTS13 results — this is a hematologic emergency
  2. Draw ADAMTS13 activity + inhibitor level BEFORE any plasma exchange or infusion
  3. DO NOT TRANSFUSE PLATELETS — provides substrate for microthrombi, can cause death
  4. Consult hematology STAT + contact blood bank/apheresis for urgent TPE
  5. Supportive care: folate 1 mg daily, RBC transfusion PRN (safe, unlike platelets), hold VTE ppx
Only exceptions for platelet transfusion: life-threatening hemorrhage (active ICH, massive GI bleed with hemodynamic instability) or required for central line placement for TPE catheter.
Evolution of TTP treatment (1924-present): mortality from >90% to <5%. Source: Front Med 2023;10:1137019, CC BY 4.0

Plasma Exchange (TPE) — The Cornerstone

Without TPE, mortality is >90%.

Plasma Exchange Mechanism: Remove antibodies + UL-vWF → Replace with functional ADAMTS13 → Platelet recovery
ParameterDetail
Mechanism(1) Removes anti-ADAMTS13 autoantibodies (2) Removes UL-vWF multimers (3) Replaces functional ADAMTS13
Dose1.0–1.5 plasma volumes/session (40–60 mL/kg). 1.5x for severe patients.
FrequencyDaily until response
Replacement fluidFFP or cryo-poor plasma. NOT albumin (contains no ADAMTS13).
AccessDouble-lumen dialysis catheter (IJ or femoral). Avoid subclavian (non-compressible + thrombocytopenia).
Response criteriaPlatelets >150K for 2 consecutive days + normalizing LDH + clinical improvement
TaperAfter response: every other day x 2–3 sessions, then stop.
TimelinePlatelets rise in 2–3 days. Median remission: 7–9 days. No response by day 5–7 = refractory.
Bridge if unavailableFFP infusion 15–30 mL/kg (provides ADAMTS13 but does NOT remove antibodies)

TPE Complications

  • Citrate toxicity: chelates Ca2+ → perioral/extremity paresthesias, cramps, QTc prolongation. Prophylax with calcium gluconate IV.
  • Catheter-related: infection (4–8%), thrombosis, pneumothorax, hemorrhage
  • Allergic reactions: urticaria, anaphylaxis. Pre-medicate: diphenhydramine + acetaminophen.
  • Hypotension / Hypothermia from volume shifts and cold plasma

Corticosteroids

ParameterDetail
TimingStart concurrently with first TPE
StandardPrednisone 1 mg/kg/day PO
Pulse (severe)Methylprednisolone 1 g IV daily x 3 days, then prednisone 1 mg/kg/day
MechanismSuppresses autoantibody production; anti-inflammatory on endothelium
DurationThrough acute treatment, then taper over 2–4 weeks
MonitoringBlood glucose, GI prophylaxis (PPI), infection risk

Caplacizumab (Cablivi)

ParameterDetail
ClassAnti-vWF nanobody (bivalent single-domain antibody fragment)
MechanismBinds vWF A1 domain, blocks vWF-platelet GPIb interaction. Immediately prevents new microthrombus formation.
Dosing11 mg IV bolus pre-TPE, then 11 mg SubQ daily (30 min post-TPE) through course + 30 days after last TPE
Key trialHERCULES (2019 NEJM): 74% reduction in composite endpoint. Faster platelet normalization. No TTP deaths in treatment arm.
GuidelinesASH 2020 + ISTH 2020: first-line for acquired TTP
Side effectsIncreased bleeding risk (epistaxis, gingival, bruising). Headache.
Cost~$270,000/course (cost-effective given reduced ICU stays/mortality)
MonitoringIf ADAMTS13 <10% when stopped → restart + add rituximab

Rituximab (Rituxan)

ParameterDetail
ClassAnti-CD20 chimeric monoclonal antibody
MechanismDepletes CD20+ B cells (precursors of antibody-producing plasma cells). Full effect: 2–4 weeks.
Dosing375 mg/m² IV weekly x 4 doses
IndicationsRefractory, relapsing, high inhibitor (>2 BU), preemptive (ADAMTS13 <10% in remission), increasingly first-line
TPE timingGive AFTER TPE (not before) — TPE removes rituximab. Give on TPE-off days or 4+ hours post-TPE.
ResponseADAMTS13 recovery to >20% in 1–3 weeks. B cell depletion lasts 6–12 months.
Side effectsInfusion reactions, HBV reactivation (screen HBsAg/anti-HBc), PML (rare), hypogammaglobulinemia
Caplacizumab works immediately (blocks vWF-platelet binding) while rituximab takes weeks (B cell depletion). They are complementary, not redundant.

Refractory TTP

Definition: No platelet recovery (remains <50K or fails to double) after 5–7 days of daily TPE + steroids.

StepInterventionDetail
1Twice-daily TPE1.5 plasma volumes BID
2Rituximab375 mg/m² weekly x 4 (if not already started)
3CaplacizumabImmediate effect (if not already started)
4BortezomibProteasome inhibitor — targets CD20-negative plasma cells. 1.3 mg/m² SubQ days 1, 4, 8, 11.
5CyclosporineCalcineurin inhibitor. 2–3 mg/kg/day, trough 150–200 ng/mL.
6Vincristine1 mg IV weekly x 3–4 doses (rarely used)
7SplenectomyLast resort. High perioperative risk in active TTP.
8N-acetylcysteineExperimental — may reduce UL-vWF size. Limited evidence.
7

Monitoring & Follow-Up

Acute inpatient monitoring transitions to lifelong outpatient ADAMTS13 surveillance. Preemptive rituximab when ADAMTS13 <10% in remission prevents relapse.
LDH = Best Daily Marker Lifelong ADAMTS13 30–50% Relapse

Inpatient Monitoring (Acute Episode)

ParameterFrequencyTarget / Notes
Platelet countDaily (BID in ICU)Trending toward >150K for 2 consecutive days
LDHDailyMost sensitive marker; falling LDH before rising platelets = good early sign
HaptoglobinEvery 1–2 daysRecovery from undetectable = late response sign
CreatinineDailyShould remain stable or improve
TroponinAt dx, then q24–48h if elevatedCardiac ischemia monitoring
Neuro examEvery 8–12 hoursDocument fluctuations; improvement expected with TPE
ADAMTS13At dx (pre-TPE), q3–5d during TPE, at remissionConfirms dx; guides treatment duration
Ionized calciumDuring/after each TPECitrate toxicity monitoring

Remission Criteria

  • Platelet count >150,000/mcL for at least 2 consecutive days
  • LDH normalizing (clearly trending down)
  • Clinical improvement (neurological symptoms resolved)
  • No evidence of ongoing hemolysis

Outpatient Surveillance Schedule

TimeframeMonitoring
Weeks 1–4CBC + LDH + haptoglobin twice weekly. Taper steroids. Complete rituximab if started.
Months 1–3CBC + LDH + ADAMTS13 monthly
Months 3–24ADAMTS13 activity every 3 months (critical surveillance period)
After 2 yearsADAMTS13 every 6–12 months indefinitely (lifelong)

Relapse Prevention

  • ADAMTS13 <10% in remission (even asymptomatic) → preemptive rituximab (375 mg/m² weekly x 4). Proven to prevent clinical relapse.
  • ADAMTS13 10–20% → monitor monthly; consider preemptive rituximab if trending down.
  • Relapse rate: 30–50% within first 2 years, highest in first 6 months.

Relapse Risk Factors

  • High inhibitor titer at diagnosis
  • Persistent low ADAMTS13 during remission
  • Black race
  • Obesity
  • High anti-ADAMTS13 IgG levels

Pregnancy Counseling

  • High relapse risk (>25–50% per pregnancy)
  • Monitor ADAMTS13 monthly throughout (start preconception if planned)
  • Hematology co-management mandatory
  • If ADAMTS13 <10%: prophylactic plasma infusions or TPE
  • Preemptive rituximab before planned pregnancy (complete 6+ months before conception)
8

Special Populations

Pregnancy, HIV, drug-induced, and congenital TTP each have unique management considerations.
Pregnancy HIV Drug-Induced Congenital

Pregnancy-Associated TTP

  • May be initial presentation of acquired or congenital TTP
  • ADAMTS13 <10% confirms TTP (vs HELLP/preeclampsia/aHUS)
  • TPE is safe during pregnancy — treatment of choice
  • Caplacizumab: limited safety data; case-by-case
  • Rituximab: crosses placenta in 2nd/3rd trimester; generally avoided
Delivery does NOT resolve TTP (unlike HELLP/preeclampsia, which improve within 48–72h). Key diagnostic and therapeutic distinction.

HIV-Associated TTP

  • Incidence 30–40x higher than general population
  • More common with low CD4 but can occur at any stage
  • Treatment: standard TTP therapy PLUS initiation/optimization of ART
  • Rituximab: use cautiously (further immunosuppression)
  • Prognosis improved in ART era; relapse lower with controlled HIV

Drug-Induced TMA

DrugADAMTS13Treatment
Ticlopidine<10% (true TTP)TPE + stop drug
ClopidogrelMay be <10%TPE + stop drug
QuinineUsually normalStop drug; TPE if severe
Cyclosporine/TacrolimusNormalDose reduce/stop
GemcitabineNormalStop; eculizumab may help
Only ticlopidine (and rarely clopidogrel) causes true ADAMTS13-deficient TTP. Other drug TMAs have normal ADAMTS13.

Congenital TTP (Upshaw-Schulman)

  • ADAMTS13 constitutively <5–10% due to biallelic mutations
  • No autoantibodies — inhibitor and IgG negative
  • Treatment: FFP infusion 10–15 mL/kg q2–3 weeks (t1/2 = 2–3 days)
  • Plasma exchange NOT necessary (no antibodies to remove)
  • Prophylactic FFP lifelong; increase during triggers
  • Recombinant ADAMTS13 (TAK-755) in clinical trials
Congenital TTP = FFP infusions, NOT plasma exchange. No antibodies to remove. Board-relevant distinction.
9

Prognosis & Clinical Pearls

Modern quad-therapy has transformed TTP from >90% mortality to <5%. These pearls are board-relevant and clinically essential.
Board Buzzwords 15 High-Yield Pearls

Prognosis by Treatment Era

TreatmentMortality
No treatment (historical)>90%
Plasma infusion alone~50%
TPE + steroids10–20%
TPE + steroids + caplacizumab~5–10%
TPE + steroids + caplacizumab + rituximab<5%
Long-term complications of iTTP by organ system. Source: Front Med 2023;10:1137019, CC BY 4.0

Board-Relevant Buzzwords

Schistocytes / Helmet Cells ADAMTS13 <10% UL-vWF Multimers Plasma Exchange NO Platelet Transfusion Fluctuating Neuro Normal PT/PTT/Fibrinogen Coombs Negative Pentad in <5%

15 High-Yield Clinical Pearls

1The diagnosis is clinical — thrombocytopenia + MAHA + no other explanation = start TPE immediately. Do not wait for ADAMTS13.
2PLASMIC ≥6 = start TPE empirically while awaiting ADAMTS13. Calculate on every suspected TMA patient.
3Normal PT / PTT / fibrinogen = NOT DIC. The most tested distinction. Only platelet plug pathway is dysfunctional in TTP.
4Coombs negative = NOT AIHA. TTP is mechanical hemolysis (shearing through platelet/vWF mesh).
5Mild renal failure = TTP. Severe (Cr >3–4, dialysis) = think HUS/aHUS. Renal severity is the best clinical discriminator.
6Fluctuating neurological symptoms = classic TTP. Confused → lucid → seizes. Dynamic cerebral microthrombi.
7Troponin elevation (25–50%) is underappreciated. Myocardial microthrombi → arrhythmias, cardiomyopathy, sudden death. Always check troponin + ECG.
8Platelet transfusion is contraindicated (rare exceptions). Adding platelets = fuel to the fire. Most commonly tested management principle.
9Draw ADAMTS13 BEFORE plasma exchange. TPE normalizes ADAMTS13, making the test uninterpretable. Common exam pitfall.
10Give rituximab AFTER TPE (not before). TPE removes rituximab from plasma.
11Caplacizumab works immediately (blocks vWF-platelet). Rituximab takes weeks (B cell depletion). Complementary, not redundant.
12ADAMTS13 monitoring is lifelong. 30–50% relapse. Preemptive rituximab when ADAMTS13 <10% in remission — before clinical relapse.
13Congenital TTP: FFP infusions, NOT plasma exchange (no antibodies to remove). Board-relevant distinction.
14Pregnancy TMA: ADAMTS13 <10% = TTP, not HELLP. HELLP improves after delivery; TTP does not.
15Untreated TTP: >90% mortality — one of the highest in medicine. True hematologic emergency requiring same-day TPE.

References

  • Joly BS, Coppo P, Veyradier A. Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura. Blood. 2017;129(21):2836-2846.

  • Zheng XL, et al. ISTH guidelines for treatment of TTP. J Thromb Haemost. 2020;18(10):2496-2502.
  • Bendapudi PK, et al. PLASMIC score for predicting severe ADAMTS13 deficiency. Lancet Haematol. 2017;4(4):e157-e164.
  • ASH 2020 guidelines on management of TTP. Blood Adv. 2020;4(20):4814-4839.
  • George JN, Nester CM. Syndromes of thrombotic microangiopathy. N Engl J Med. 2014;371(7):654-666.
  • Kremer Hovinga JA, et al. Pathophysiology, diagnosis, and treatment of TTP. Blood. 2017;129(21):2836-2846.
  • Sayani FA, Abrams CS. How I treat refractory TTP. Blood. 2015;125(25):3860-3867.
